1: The chapter opens with the death of Nahash king of the Ammonites — the same Nahash whose defeat by Saul had established the latter's kingship (1 Sam 11). David's declaration, ʾeʿĕśeh ḥesed ʿim-Ḥānûn ben-Nāḥāš kî-ʿāśāh ʾāḇîw ḥesed ʿimmādî — “I will show ḥesed to Hanun son of Nahash, because his father showed ḥesed to me” — frames the mission in covenant-loyalty vocabulary. ḥesed denotes the reciprocal obligation of treaty partners; David's initiative honors the alliance established with the late king.
2: David sends a delegation lᵉnaḥămô — to comfort/console him (nāḥam piel). The verb is the standard one for mourning consolation (cf. Gen 37:35; 2 Sam 12:24). That David sends officials (ʿăḇādîm, lit. servants) to perform this duty rather than going himself reflects ancient Near Eastern protocol for inter-royal mourning diplomacy. The mission is cast as an act of covenant maintenance (ḥesed) carrying political implications.
3: Hanun's advisors interpret the delegation with suspicion: hălᵉḵabbēd ʾet-ʾāḇîḵā bᵉʿênêḵā šālaḥ lᵉḵā dāwid mᵉnaḥᵉmîm — “Do you think David is honoring your father in your eyes that he sent comforters to you?” The verb kābad (piel: to honor/treat as weighty) is used sarcastically. Their accusation: hălōʾ bîᵉʿăḇûr laḥqōr wᵉlahapoḵ wᵉlaraggēl — “Did they not come to spy, to overthrow, and to spy out?” The threefold verb series — ḥāqar (investigate/spy), hāpaḵ (overturn), rāgal (spy on the land) — expresses escalating hostility.
4: Hanun's response — wayyiqqaḥ Ḥānûn ʾet-ʿaḇdê dāwid waygallaḥ wayyikrat ʾet-madwêhem baḥᵉṣî ʿad-hašōq — he seized the men, shaved them (implying beard-removal, a severe humiliation in ANE culture), and cut their garments at the buttock. The shaving of the beard was a form of public shaming; the exposure of the lower body (šōq) added sexual humiliation. This act violated diplomatic immunity and constituted a declaration of war. The parallel in 2 Sam 10:4 is identical.
5: David sends word to the men: šᵉḇû ḇîrêḥô ʿad ʾăšer yiṣmaḥ zᵉqanḵem — “Remain at Jericho until your beards have grown.” The beard-growth instruction confirms the interpretation of v4's shaving as the insult. Jericho, at the Jordan's edge, is the natural first city entering Canaan from the east — the men can recuperate there without the further humiliation of appearing at the capital. David's response is measured and protective rather than immediately retaliatory.
6: The Ammonites recognize they have made themselves odious (nib ʾāšû — they had made themselves a stench, from bāʾaš, to stink/be abhorrent). They hire mercenaries: wayyišlᵉḥû banê ʿAmmôn wayyiśkᵉrû ʾet-ʾArām Naharayim wᵉʾet-ʾArām Maʿăḵāh wᵉʾet-Ṣôḇāh ʾalpayim ûšlōšîm rekeḇ — 32,000 chariots from Aram Naharaim, Aram Maacah, and Zobah. The figure differs from 2 Sam 10:6 (33,000 foot soldiers). The specific regions — Aram Naharaim (“Aram of the two rivers,” = upper Mesopotamia), Maacah (south of Damascus), and Zobah (central Syria) — represent the major Aramean powers of the period.
7: wayyiśkᵉrû ʾălêhem šᵉlōšîm ûšᵉnayim ʾelep rekeḇ — 32,000 chariots and the king of Maacah with his troops. They camp at Mêḏᵉḇāʾ (Medeba, east of the Dead Sea in Transjordan). The Ammonites themselves mustered from their cities. The geographical deployment — Aramean chariots in open country at Medeba, Ammonites holding the city entrance — establishes the tactical challenge Joab will face in v10.
8: David sends Joab with all the host of mighty warriors (kol-ṣᵉḇāʾ haggibbōrîm). The term gibbōrîm (mighty men, warriors) likely refers to the elite corps developed under David (cf. 2 Sam 23:8-39; 1 Chr 11:10-47). Joab's deployment with the full professional army signals David's recognition of the seriousness of the Aramean coalition.
9: The Ammonites position themselves petaḥ hāʿîr — at the city entrance (gateway of Medeba). The Aramean kings are positioned separately in the open field (baśśādeh). This two-front positioning — urban Ammonites + open-field Arameans — forces Joab to divide his forces. The tactical situation closely parallels the 2 Samuel 10 account.
10: Joab perceives (wayyarᵉ Yôʾāḇ kî-hāytāh pᵉnê hammilḥāmāh ʾēlāyw mippānîm ûmēʾāḥôr) that the battle fronts face him from front and behind. His response — dividing the force — follows classic Israelite battle doctrine when facing encirclement. He selects elite troops (mibḥar bᵉyiśrāʾēl) for the Aramean engagement and deploys his brother Abishai for the Ammonite front.
11: Joab places his chosen troops (mibḥar bᵉyiśrāʾēl — the choicest of Israel) against Aram and assigns the rest to Abishai his brother (ʾāḥîw). The division reflects tactical judgment: Aramean chariots in open field require the most capable unit; the Ammonites at the city gate can be held by the remainder.
12: Joab's tactical speech: ʾim-teḥĕzaq mimmenî ʾărām wᵉhāyîtā lî litᵉšûʿāh — “If Aram is too strong for me, you will come to my aid” — and vice versa. The mutual support arrangement is a practical contingency plan. The speech ends with the formula: ḥăzaq wᵉnitḥăzzᵉqāh bᵉʿad-ʿammênû ûbᵉʿad ʿārê ʾĕlōhênû — “Be strong (ḥāzaq, qal) and let us show ourselves strong (ḥāzaq, hithpael) for our people and for the cities of our God.”
13: The battle cry formula: ḥăzaq wᵉnitḥăzzᵉqāh — the qal imperative followed by the hithpael cohortative of ḥāzaq (to be/become strong). This pairing of simple command with reflexive/intensive form is used elsewhere in high-stakes leadership moments: Moses to Joshua (Deut 31:6-7), Joshua to Israel (Josh 1:6-9), David to Solomon (1 Chr 22:13). The conclusion — wᵉYHWH yaʿăśeh haṭṭôḇ bᵉʿênāyw — “YHWH will do what seems good in his eyes” — is a radical trust statement: the outcome is submitted to divine discretion after human effort is expended.
14: Joab draws near (wayyiqraḇ Yôʾāḇ wᵉhāʿām ʾăšer ʿimmô) to engage Aram, and they flee before him (wayyānuśû mippānāyw). The Aramean flight is emphatic — the coalition that seemed so formidable collapses at the first engagement. The same verb nûs (to flee) appears in v15 for the Ammonites when they see the Arameans fleeing.
15: When the Ammonites see (wayyirᵉʾû) that Aram had fled, they themselves flee before Abishai. The sequence — Arameans flee first, then Ammonites flee when they see it — reflects the psychological collapse of a coalition. The Ammonites enter the city (wayyāḇōʾû hāʿîr); Joab returns to Jerusalem. The campaign ends inconclusively for the cities — Rabbah is not taken yet (see ch20).
16: The Aramean defeat at Medeba triggers a wider mobilization: wayyirᵉʾû ʾărām kî nig ʾăḵû lipnê yiśrāʾēl wayyišlᵉḥû malʾāḵîm wayyôṣiʾû ʾet-ʾărām ʾăšer mēʿēḇer hannāhār — seeing they were defeated, they send for Arameans from beyond the River (Euphrates). Šôḇaḵ śar-ṣᵉḇāʾ Hadadezer — Shophach, commander of Hadadezer's army (spelled Shobach in 2 Sam 10:16). The variant spelling in Chronicles suggests an alternate tradition or textual transmission.
17: David is told (wayyuggad lᵉḏāwid) and he gathers all Israel, crosses the Jordan (wayyaʿăḇor hayYardēn), and comes upon them (wayyāḇōʾ ʾălêhem). The Jordan crossing signals a major campaign — not a border skirmish but an expeditionary force into Transjordan. David positions (wayyaʿărok ʾălêhem) for battle; the Arameans take up formation against Israel.
18: The Arameans flee before Israel: wayyānos ʾărām mippᵉnê yiśrāʾēl wayyahrog dāwid mēʾărām šiḇʿat ʾălāpîm rekeḇ — David kills 7,000 of Aram's charioteers (rekeḇ, chariot-riders) and 40,000 foot soldiers. The 2 Sam 10:18 parallel says “700 charioteers” — the Chronicler's tenfold higher figure may reflect different counting conventions or textual transmission. Shophach the commander is also killed.
19: The vassal Aramean kings who had served Hadadezer make peace with David: wayyirᵉʾû ʿaḇdê Hadadezer kî nugᵉpû lipnê yiśrāʾēl wayyašlimû ʿim dāwid — “the servants of Hadadezer saw they were struck down before Israel and made peace (šālam hiphil) with David.” The hiphil of šālam (to be whole, complete, at peace) here means to make a treaty of submission. The result: wᵉʾet-ʾAmmon lōʾ ʾāḇāh ʿāzōr — the Arameans would no longer help the Ammonites, leaving them isolated for the final campaign of ch20.
